key_insight: Including dynamic electrostatic disorder in nonadiabatic simulations significantly changes rubrene hole transport, making the predicted mobility lower and more realistic.
engineering_relevance: For organic semiconductor design, this shows that long-range electrostatic effects can materially affect mobility predictions and should not be neglected in high-mobility crystal modeling.
method: Used fragment orbital-based surface hopping (FOB-SH) nonadiabatic molecular dynamics for room-temperature hole transport in rubrene.
Included dynamic electrostatics with the damped shifted-force (DSF) real-space summation plus an addition-subtraction scheme.
Compared transport with and without electrostatics to quantify changes in reorganization energy, disorder, and mobility.
evidence_signal: The simulations showed increased reorganization energy, reduced inverse participation ratio from 13 to 9, and mobility along the high-mobility direction dropped from 35 to 21 cm^2 V^-1 s^-1, matching experiment closely.
watch_item: DSF electrostatics improve realism but add computational cost, so scalability and sensitivity to model assumptions should be checked for larger or less ordered crystals.
paper_title: Impact of dynamic electrostatic disorder on hole mobility in rubrene: a nonadiabatic molecular dynamics investigation
